title: Poor housing quality and the health of newborns and young children
abstract: This study uses linked administrative data on live births, hospital stays, and census records for children born in Hungary between 2006 and 2011 to examine the relationship between poor housing quality and the health of newborns and children aged 1-2 years. We show that poor housing quality, defined as lack of access to basic sanitation and exposure to polluting heating, is not a negligible problem even in a high-income EU country like Hungary. This is particularly the case for disadvantaged children, 20-25% of whom live in extremely poor-quality homes. Next, we provide evidence that poor housing quality is strongly associated with lower health at birth and a higher number of days spent in inpatient care at the age of 1-2 years. These results indicate that lack of access to basic sanitation, hygiene, and non-polluting heating and their health impacts cannot be considered as the exclusive problem for low- and middle-income countries. In high-income countries, there is also a need for public policy programs that identify those affected by poor housing quality and offer them potential solutions to reduce the adverse effects on their health.
summary_en: Even in a high-income EU country like Hungary, 20–25% of disadvantaged children grow up without basic sanitation or in homes heated by polluting fuels. This paper uses linked administrative records — births, hospital stays, and census housing data — for Hungarian children born 2006–2011 and shows that poor housing quality is strongly associated with worse health at birth and more inpatient-care days at ages 1–2. The finding pushes back against the idea that housing quality is a concern only for low- and middle-income countries: inside high-income EU members, pockets of poor housing have measurable child-health consequences.
data_used: Hungarian administrative data for children born 2006–2011: live-birth registers, inpatient-care records (OEP/NEAK), and census housing data; linked at the individual level; several hundred thousand children.
policy_relevance: Strong case for Hungarian Ministry of Interior (housing portfolio) and Ministry of Construction: basic-sanitation and heating upgrades in poor-quality housing deliver measurable child-health gains. Relevant to EU cohesion funds for housing upgrades (e.g., IZOTÓP programme) and targeted social-housing reconstruction. Hungarian administrative data; results directly applicable, though long-run (beyond age 2) effects are yet to be tracked.
